turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments first this morning in Edwards v. Arizona. Mr. Meehan, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court:

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How old was he?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: He was 23 at•that time, Your Honor. He was 24 at the time of the trial, which was more than a year later. At the Police Department he was read his Miranda rights, which he said he understood. He gave a recorded state­ ment at the Tucson Police Department, when interrogated by a police detective named Bunting. At that time his statement was denying any implication in the offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he had already been formally charged at that time?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: He had been formally charged, we con­ tend, and the State had agreed with us in the lower courts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, as I understood their brief, they agree with you here. give him a chance to tell his side of the story but they would not entertain any kind of deal. Ultimately, Edwards was taken for holding overnight at the Pima County jail after he told Detective Bunting in words to the effect: I'm not going to make a statement now, I'm not going to make a deal now, I'm going to wait until I get my attorney.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think forever after he would be -- that no statement would be admissible?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: "Forever after" is a long time,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's what I had in mind.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I think that "forever after" until the next day at 1:30 when he was given a first arraignment, or your position.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: My position is that in terms of applica­ tion of Miranda, Mr. Chief Justice, that unless truly the accused himself institutes a change of mind, that there should be no reinterrogation until counsel has a chance to confer with the accused who has asked for it. This Court made very clear, I think, in Miranda, that that situation, where an accused asks .;.for a lawyer., should be treated differently from the situation where the accused simply listens to the rights and then goes ahead and speaks or even listens to the rights being read to him and decides to remain silent, which indicates that he is feeling capable of making his own deci­ sions about whether to deal with the interrogating officers or not to deal with them. It was said in Miranda and this Court confirmed in 20 January, hours before Edwards would be making his first appearance and at a time when the police were aware that Edwards as an indigent would be appointed counsel that after­ noon. They knew that he was about to get a lawyer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it could be, Mr. Meehan, that if a crime occurs at 11 o'clock at night, that there aren't many lawyers willing to get out of bed at that hour of night to provide the necessary support for a defendant, that might be during the daytime.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: That's true, Mr. Justice Rehnquist. And, indeed, in Miranda, what was said was, we're not holding that the police have to have a lawyer in every precinct. But warnings and affording the right to counsel is affecting him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you say the case was solved, was it solved as to Edwards?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it certainly won't be, if you have your way in this Court. I mean, Edwards will go free, will he not ?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I think that's an assumption that can't be made at this point, because at the trial the evidence in­ cluded the testimony of the police officers, but also testimony of an accomplice named Cleveland Reed. There was also obtained, and before this interrogation.of Edwards, a tape-recorded con­ fession of another accomplice named Soto. As a matter of fact that tape recorded confession had been played, several minutes As I said, in the courts below and in its briefs in this Court, except the supplemental brief, the State has agreed with the Petitioner that at the time of this interrogation formal prosecution had begun and the right to counsel under the Sixth Amendment as a critical stage had arisen. A week ago the State contended on the virtue of a Second Circuit case construing federal criminal rules of proce­ dure that no formal prosecution had begun. I think the State was correct in its first position. In Arizona, under the criminal rules, prosecution may be commenced in two ways, either by indictment or by complaint. In this case prosecutior was commenced by the filing of a complaint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But doesn't the complaint require that the county attorney take it to the court and file it?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: The complaint is presented to a magis­ January. the magistrate. Now, in Arizona the probable cause proceedings and the complaint are begun before a magistrate, which typi­ cally is a police court or a justice court, as it is in Arizona. And ultimately, those documents are then filed in the prosecution file in the Court of General Jurisdiction, which is the Superior Court. And that filing did occur some­ what after the day of the interrogation. But in terms of the magistrate receiving the complaint and having the officer appear before him and taking sworn testimony if he chooses to do so, and so forth, this was done on the 19th of January. where in the respondent's original brief there is a concession that your client had been formally charged at the time of this interrogation ?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know I read it but I can't find it.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: It's Footnote 47 on page 24. The respondent recognizes the efficacy of Massiah and also cites without dispute the McLeod case of this Court, which was important. I would say that the distinction is important in terms of the due process argument that we make, and also in terms of the motivation of the interrogating officers at this point. I think it's fair to say that knowing that the petitioner would have counsel appointed for him that after­ noon and knowing that any counsel worth his salt is going to fully apprise this defendant of what he is charged with and what the severity of the charges are and what the police can do with a statement and so forth is going to advise this defen­ dant, not now to try quickly to make a deal by giving a state­ ment or to make a statement at all. And of course, that's something that is to me, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr: Meehan, .did the proceeding before the magistrate actually begin on the 19th?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was it completed that day? hearing. Arizona has kind of a strange term for it. We used to call it "a one-man grand jury" because it can be a very full-blown hearing or it can be a very perfunctory hearing in which the magistrate having placed before him a rather routine felony complaint and having no basis to disagree with what's sworn to by the officer simply says, yes, and, I'll issue the complaint. As an example, one of the other ways that this rule has been used in times before the middle 70s when Arizona be­ gan to use grand juries with regularity, the so-called one-man grand jury was used to investigate corruption in public office We had a sheriff in Pima County who was rather famous, indeed, I would say, notorious, whose prosecution was originally begun with the earlier procedural equivalent of this Rule 2.2, 2.3, and 2.4 procedure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, has that superseded entirely the old procedure where the defendant after the warrant of arrest involved with public preliminary hearings at which the accused is present, a possibility of a complaint beginning a case and then an indictment superseding the complaint.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that didn't happen?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: That did happen in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The indictment did supersede?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: The indictment did supersede.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what was the date of the indictment?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I think, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, but I'm not sure, that it was the 27th or so of January. It was fairly shortly after, but it was after the interrogation. I should point out one problem with getting into this area of formal prosecution. Edwards was arrested in 1976. He was not tried until April of 1977, and in the interim the charges against him were dismissed because of procedural problems not germane ask the Clerk to supplement that if that becomes necessary or appropriate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your position is not that the police cannot at the scene of a crime interrogate witnesses whom they think might be guilty, unless each and every one of the wit­ nesses has a lawyer, is it?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: No, indeed. But that's investigative interrogation and quite different, I think, from this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it depends on the motive of the police?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Not motive entirely. I think motive is important in deciding what is appropriate and what is not appropriate. I think that motive is important here because, number one, there are no countervailing considerations to re­ quire prompt interrogation. If there were some other problem, easily divisible into the category that I'm describing is the fact of the complaint, and the police believing that, they'd been working on the case for 15 months, they believe now they've got enough, they've gotten two statements, the time between the offense and the arrest, and so forth, I think sim­ ply tells them at that time and tells this Court at this time that we're dealing with a post-complaint, a post-indictment -- to use a shorthand term -- interrogation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Meehan: I didn't understand one of your answers to Justice Rehnquist. Supposing you're right at the scene of the crime and the police ask somebody who was a suspect a question, they've let him know he's a suspect; he says, I want a lawyer. I don't want to answer your question. Is it your view they can go ahead and question him anyway?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I would say that if the suspect is in custody it would be a consideration. to the Fifth Amendment. I think that unless this Court is going to change what I believe has been clearly stated in Miranda and the other cases that you're bringing out that at that point the police have to scrupulously honor that request and not reinterrogate until a lawyer is provided. At that point --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that's based on Miranda, whereas the facts here are Massiah -- ah, Sixth Amendment —

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I think that this is more a Sixth Amendment, Massiah -- or really, a Brewer type of case than it is a Miranda type of case, although obviously the two really come very close together.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In Miranda, for Miranda to help him, he would have to be in custody, would he not?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, did he get this notice on the 20th?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: The record indicates that he was shown a copy of the arrest warrant at the time he was arrested on the 19th, shown in the sense, I am sure, of not handing it to him to read but to support the arrest, because there is an in­ dication that there was a scuffle at the time of the arrest. He was told generally that he was an accused in a murder case c n the 19th when he was interrogated by Sergeant Bunting at the Tucson Police Department. In terms of explaining to him pre­ were, precisely where they were in stage of proceedings and so forth, the record doesn't show that he was given that in­ formation at any time before he made the statement on the sufficient. All other waivers of trial rights, rights like the right to counsel, which involved the factfinding process, the fairness of the trial, and the reliability of the trial, have always been required to be made in open court and approved and supervised by a trial judge, so far as I can tell. This Court has said in in the Schneckloth case that the high standard of waiver which I think applies in the Sixth Amendment kinds of cases requires a showing of knowledge, understanding, and voluntary relinquishment that can only, or at least is de­ signed to be carried out in that kind of a setting. I submit tion for finding a waiver of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel. It's possible that in order to establish the very high standard that this Court has imposed by Johnson v. Zerbst and by Fare v. Michael C., and by at least in dictum Schneckloth, and Faretta v. California for waiver of right to this Court needs to accept or reject any one of the three or that even any one of the three necessarily have to be exclu­ sive, but it seems to me that the cases of this Court impose a very high standard of joined waiver and that in order to fulfill that some kind of quite reliable factfinding process needs to be undertaken before there can be a waiver. In this case, what do we have that would establish a waiver? Number one, when the police went to the jail on the morning of the 20th to interrogate Edwards, he said he didn't want to come out of "the hole," as he described it. Number two, after he was given his Miranda rights and was read a tape recording of another accomplice, he made the very conflicting statement, I'll tell you what you want to hear, but I don't want it recorded because' I' don't want it used against me in interrogation started?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I haven't focused on it that clearly, Your Honor, in terms of after Edwards was brought into the interview or interrogation room at the jail. They read him his rights, they said they wanted to talk with him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, specifically, do you contend that playing him the tape of the other person's confession was the commencement of interrogation under Rhode Island v. Innis?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Yes, I think it was, because I think it was done whether there was a waiver at that point or later. Tb answer interrogation started. after the tape was played. If the Court please, I would like to reserve the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Meehan. Mr. McClennen.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court: This case involves two issues, admissibility of a con­ fession after a request for an attorney, and the waiver of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel. The State feels that there are several important facts to be considered in this case. First is, Mr. Edwards was warned of his right four That was four times. On the night of January 19, after the discussion, after the tape recorded statement, and after the discussion back and forth about the deal, Mr. Edwards never said, "I do not want to talk to you again until I see an attorney." He never said, "I will not answer any more questions till I see an attorney." What he said was, "I will wait until I get an attor­ ney before I make a deal. I'll wait till then before I make a statement."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where do you find that particular dia­ logue? Is that 70-71?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: That's several pages throughout the record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's on 71: "Well, I need a lawyer."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where did he talk about making the deal? the cross-examination starts: "He . . . stated that without a deal he was not going to make a statement. He would wait for his attorney to make a deal for him."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Attorney General, where did he ever say, "I withdraw my request for a lawyer"?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: He never specifically said that. The next morning when Detective Marmion went there, Detective Marmion -- page 57 of the Appendix, towards the bottom: "A. I got there. I identified myself to Mr. Barefield as a homicide detective. He said something to the effect, I don’t remember his exact words, that he recognized me from seeing me the night before at the Dectective Division. I told him I was the detective that had the case and I wanted to talk to him. I then advised him of his constitutional rights. He replied that he A. And also the fact that you had the right to an attorney. You did not request one at that time."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But he at no time withdrew his request?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: He never said, I will no longer wait until I get an attorney to make a deal .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How did we get a waiver without that?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: They advised him of his rights. He said, I understand that and I'll answer your questions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's it.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: They said, you have the right to I understand that, I'll answer your questions. Now, in North Carolina v. Butler, it is said that you do not have to have a specific waiver of the right to an attorney. You must look to the facts of the case. We submit that this dialogue where he was specifically told that he had as a witness in behalf of the defendant." Are those both motions to suppress in the Superior Court?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: No, Justice Rehnquist, the proceed­ ings, March 4 and March 7, those are the proceedings at the suppression hearing. Then, the proceedings, March 21 and 28, the latter part of March, that is the first trial, the one that ended in a hung jury and a mistrial. The proceedings in April were the second trial where Mr. Edwards was convicted. Now, the next morning, when Detective Marmion went to visit Mr. Edwards, the reason why Detective Marmion went to been assigned as the lead detective in that case. It was his responsibility. The reason why Detective Marmion did not arrest Mr. Edwards, Marmion and Milne were in the process of arresting Manny Soto while Sergeant Bunting and Detective first I want to hear Manny Soto's tape. Detective Marmion played the tape per Mr. Edwards' request, and that caused Mr. Edwards to confess. The tape --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where in the record is it that says that that caused him to confess?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Nothing says that that caused him to confess. But they played the tape --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's your conclusion?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: That's my conclusion. They played the tape and immediately he says, all right, I'll give you my story.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the record tell us how Edwards knew they had Soto's tape?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: No. That's unclear. He knew on the night of the 19th that Mr. Soto was being interrogated but Our position in this matter is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How soon after he heard that tape did he begin to talk and make these statements?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: According to the record it was imme­ diately after that. He heard the tape --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did he -- what statement did he make to introduce his statement after he heard the tape recording of the accomplice?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: I'm not sure where in the record it says that after he heard the tape he said, all right, I'll make a statement. It's somewhere, but I can't put my finger on it right now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are defending the judgment of your court and the rationale of it?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that you have to contend there doesn't have to be an express waiver?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or a written waiver, a fortiori?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Or a written waiver under --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just the mere fact of being willing to answer questions?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: In this situation, yes, where he was told he had the right to an attorney, he knew he had the right would stop talking —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So suppose a man in custody is given his Miranda warnings and then the officer just asks him some

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: You can make the argument that once you've invoked it, you know that you have it, that clearly indicates within the person's mind that he has those rights and what will happen if he invokes them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is that true about -- that might be true about silence, but how about the attorney? You know, when you invoke your right to an attorney you're in a sense saying, I'm not competent to handle my own affairs.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Maybe.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say, silence; maybe you are. that when a person says, I wish to remain silent, that he is controlling his own destiny. But if he says, I want an attorney, it indicates that he is acknowledging he is

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know but your Supreme Court, and you're defending that, said he invoked his right to counsel --

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- before he was to do anything.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right, go ahead. So when he heard the tape, he just said something. Is that it?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Well, first, they read him his rights. And he said, all right, I'll answer your questions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but didn't your Supreme Court also recognize that when the officers arrived the next morning and the jailor said to him, the officers are here, he said, I don't want to talk to anyone? And didn't the jailor say, yes, but

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes. That is in the record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about your Supreme Court?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Well, this court can look at the same facts --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I just asked you, how about your Supreme Court? What'd the Supreme Court -- ?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: The Supreme Court did point out that fact.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And -- so that he was told that he had to?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: They quoted from the record. waiver simply from his willingness to talk thereafter.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wouldn’t it follow from your logic that every time there’s a violation of the protections of Miranda there's a waiver of them?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It sounds to me as though.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: In this case the police --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whenever he does talk, then he's waived all right, I'll answer your questions. There's a waiver then?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's perfectly consistent with your logic, isn't it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, it is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He's told that he may remain silent and he: says, maybe one night or maybe the next morning, I know I have the right to remain silent, but I'm going to talk.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he didn't say that here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's the point.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Well, maybe that's a waiver, but that's not what you have here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's turn to page 71 of your Appendix, At the bottom of 71 he said, "Well, I need a lawyer." And then I'll read his statement: He -- this is Edwards the story." Is that language what you rely on to make the waiver?

turns.name: MCCLENNAN
turns.text: Not for the statement on the 20th. That preceded the tape recorded statement on the 19th. The waiver on the 20th --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have to get to that, don't you?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you say this is or is not a waiver, on the 19th? On the bottom of the page, the colloquy on the bottom of page 71?

turns.name: MCCLENNAN
turns.text: Well, I think that would be a waiver. But nobody's challenging the admissibility of the statement on the 19th.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But do you suggest it has no relation to what happened on the 20th?

turns.name: MCCLENNAN
turns.text: Well, it indicates that he under­ stands his rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that he wants to talk to you. Do you say it indicates he wants to talk to the police?

turns.name: MCCLENNAN
turns.text: Yes. turn on a record and he listens to it and then he says some­ thing, that's a waiver?

turns.name: MECLENNEN
turns.text: I'm not sure whether that would be a waiver but that's not what we have here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why isn't that what happened here?

turns.name: MECLENNEN
turns.text: Because what happened here is, he asked for counsel, and he was sent back to the jail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And your Supreme Court recognized that?

turns.name: MECLENNEN
turns.text: The next morning the police came.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes;

turns.name: MECLENNEN
turns.text: The officer introduced himself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MECLENNEN
turns.text: Read him his rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Our position is that he changed his mind.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he changed his mind after the jailor said, you have to talk. But you just don't -- you say that we must, or should, ignore that interchange between him and the j ailor.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if we don't? What if we don't?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Well, what happens then -- of course, Mr. Edwards, when he testified, said he told the detectives he said that. And the judge believed the detectives, believed that Edwards never told the detectives --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he may not have but he certainly was told by the jailor. concluded that' it was- a voluntary waiver?

turns.name: MCCLENNAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the jailor dispute the statement?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Did what?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did the jailor dispute the statement? MR. McCLENNEN The jailor was never called to tes- tify.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was there any reason why he couldn't have been called? MR. McCLENNEN There is no reason in the record indicated.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: They told him -- again they read the rights. You have the right to remain silent. You have the right to an attorney. You have the right to an attorney with you right now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What happens then, what is his right? When he asks for an attorney, does that change the legal situa­ tion at all and if so, how?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: It's our position --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What duty, if any, does it impose upon the police?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: If he says --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He said, I want a lawyer. Now, what duty did that impose on the police?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: They cannot question him further -- Miranda warnings and he said, I want a lawyer. Then they give him his Miranda warnings again --

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: And he says, I'll answer your ques­ tions .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He says, I'll talk.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: But first I want to hear the tape. And they accommodate him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Supposing: they give him his Miranda warnings and he says, I want a lawyer, but before I get the lawyer, I want to tell you my side of the story?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Then that would be a selective waiver The man said, yes, but with an attorney. The policeman didn't stop; he said, do you want to talk to me without an attorney? The man said, all right. The 9th Circuit said, that's all right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that' s not this case . I asked you if ther was anything in the record that said that and you said, no. HR. McCLENNEN: Said what?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That he could, waive his right.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: There's nothing, where he specifi­ cally says, all right, I will talk to you without an attorney. But they tell him he specifically has the'right Ito remain silent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, suppose, the night before, after they stopped• questioning ; him, they took him in another room and this new officer came in and gave him his warnings? Now, here's the question.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: I guess the man --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, once the man says I want a lawyer, he wants a lawyer. change his mind. regulations, once a man asks for a lawyer you stop questioning him. That's the FBI regulations.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Right. But that's not Rodriguez- Gastelum.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't think those are good regulations do you? They do a pretty good job.

turns.name: MCCLENNAN
turns.text: Well, yes. At any rate, the cases we have cited that I have mentioned say that once the person has asked for an attorney, he can go back and question him before he has seen an attorney. So, our position, that's what and because they read him a statement, Soto's statement, he decided he might as well talk.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. McClennan, then, in your primary brief you state in Footnote 47, on page 24, that the Respon­ dent State acknowledges that the petitioner was formally charged by the State on January 19, 1976. Have you changed and then it said, "adversary judicial proceedings." it said "judicial criminal proceedings." And in : Moore v. Illinois, in five places it said, "adversary ..judit- cial criminal proceedings.." It happens that the complaint is not an adversary proceeding. A policeman can file a complaint, a citizen off the street can file a complaint --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, of course, every State is different. And New York is certainly different from Arizona. And I had understood that you had conceded that in your State and under your procedures, in Arizona, the petitioner had been formally charged on the date that you conceded he had been charged.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Well, I conceded that the State had filed a complaint. But the question is, does that begin the adversary judicial criminal proceedings?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you conceded that it did in your

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did the Supreme Court of Arizona indicate about -- ,

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: It never said anything on that issue, and another matter is that at the motion to suppress it was never argued that the confession should have been suppressed because the Sixth Amendment had started. That issue sort of cropped up as this came along.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: At this point, Mr. McClellan, just so I understand the State procedure. I understand they did super­ sede with a new indictment --

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- but say they had not filed an indict­ ment. Would it have been permissible without filing any other piece of paper with the court to go to trial?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it's your present position. It wasn't your position in your brief.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: No, it's our position --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And all through this litigation it hasn't been your position, as I understand it.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: No. But we are trying to establish what the law is right now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how do we know what the Arizona law is? We must rely on you. You told us in your opening brief that he had been formally charged on the date mentioned.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'm ndt sure. Is that really right1: You didn't necessarily -- as I read the footnote, you just con­ ceded that he was entitled to an attorney.

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Maybe he's entitled to an attorney even into each other, but as a matter of constitutional law they're quite distinct. HR. McCLENNAN: Yes. What's interesting in Miranda, at page 469, the Court says, "You must tell him that what he says can be used against him because he's faced with A phase of the adversary system." And at page 477, it again says, "It is at this point that our adversary system of criminal procedure commences." Now, that's under Miranda. But it's our position that the filing of the complaint does not begin the adversary judicial It's not an adversary proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, even if it were, do you say that -- assume that it was, would you say Massiah takes over if an attorney hasn't been appointed yet?

turns.name: MCCLENNEN
turns.text: Right. However, in this case, we contend they didn't start then. It's also our position, even if they did start, he waived them. Because he knew he was charged with a crime. They showed him a copy of the arrest warrant. Sergeant Bunting discussed the charges with him -- Mr. Edwards, on page 101 and 102 of the brief -- "I advised him of the charge and showed him a copy of the warrant." And at page 104: "Q. Did you have any other conversations...? charges." He knew that he was charged with a crime, he knew that he had the right to an attorney; he had previously been

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: One or two items, if I may. The sectior. of the record that you're inquiring about which I think most clearly expresses Edwards's statement in asking for a lawyer is on page 151. Now, that may be moot because the State --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of the Appendix?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Of the Appendix. That may be moot be­ cause the State has agreed with me that on the 19th, as the trial court found and as the Arizona Supreme Court found, The Arizona Supreme Court didn't speak to the com­ mencement of formal prosecution because of the concession,

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: That's true, but the question—

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or if it was, it was done so in silence.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I would agree with that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or it may have decided that it wasn't relevant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, with a concession by the State in the Supreme Court of Arizona that he had been formally charged with an offense, I would think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the Arizona Supreme court was con­ scious of the law that your friend has just mentioned that the complaint does not formally commence the proceeding, then the Arizona Supreme Court may have concluded that that was the law without discussing it.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: I would acknowledge that that is con­ ceivable but I think that counsel does not accurately state the law because the filing of the information follows, for statute or the Constitution that the criminal proceeding is, must be, commenced by either the information or an indictment.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: If the Court please, the constitutional provision does not say it must be commenced that way. It says that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what was he reading from, do you recall?

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: Article II, Section 30, of the Arizona Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What does that tell us? I see you have it. Massiah does not apply because here the right to counsel had attached but counsel had not actually been either retained or afforded. And this Court again sent the case back.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It just reversed, didn't it? MR."MEEHAN:: It reversed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And citing Massiah.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: MEEHAN
turns.text: : Yes. Obviously, it's not a plenary opinion.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen. The case is submitted. (whereupon, at 11:02 o'clock a.m., the case in the above-entitled matter was submitted.) State of Arizona proceedings for the records of the Court.
